sources.notes: Read the complete current statement, cyclic-shift definition in all three directions, samples, and all constraints: t <= 100, a,b,c <= 3e6, abc <= 3e6, k <= 1e6, positive sorted color counts summing to abc, and sum k <= 1e6.

Read the full 2040F section and both official accepted implementations, Solution Phi and Solution DP. They group translations by the lcm of their coordinate orders, count fixed colorings by a multinomial, and either weight divisor triples with Euler phi or aggregate exact orders with a three-layer lcm-state DP.

Actually read the complete independent write-up. It derives Burnside over the three-dimensional translation group, proves the coordinate-order and Euler-phi counts, gives the fixed-coloring multinomial, and implements the divisor-triple formula.

Independently proved the orbit-length and fixed-point formula. Exact enumeration of colorings and translation orbits matched the formula on 53 two- and three-color cases with at most eight cells, including 2x2x2; a modular implementation also reproduced all six samples.

annotation.summary: Count colorings of an a by b by c toroidal grid with exact per-color multiplicities, identifying two colorings when one is a translation of the other in the three coordinate directions.
annotation.constraints: There are t <= 100 tests; 1 <= a,b,c <= 3e6, N=abc <= 3e6, 1 <= k <= 1e6, 1 <= d_1 <= ... <= d_k <= 3e6, sum d_i=N, and sum k over tests is at most 1e6. The modulus is 998244353, so N is invertible modulo the modulus.
annotation.core_idea: Apply Burnside to G=Z_a x Z_b x Z_c, the N translations. For a translation (x,y,z), its coordinate orders are p=a/gcd(a,x), q=b/gcd(b,y), and r=c/gcd(c,z), so the translation has order L=lcm(p,q,r). Repeatedly applying it partitions all N cells into N/L cycles, every one of length L. A fixed coloring must be constant on each cycle, hence exists exactly when L divides every d_i; then its count is (N/L)! divided by the product of (d_i/L)!. For every p dividing a, exactly phi(p) values of x have coordinate order p, and similarly for b and c. Therefore sum phi(p)phi(q)phi(r) times that multinomial over p|a, q|b, r|c with L|gcd(d_1,...,d_k), then divide by N. Group equal L values before evaluating the O(k) multinomial.
annotation.complexity: With M=3e6 and tau(n) denoting the divisor count, O(M log M) one-time preprocessing for factorials, inverse factorials, and the displayed phi sieve. Per test, O(sqrt(a)+sqrt(b)+sqrt(c)+tau(a)tau(b)tau(c)+k tau(g)) arithmetic with hash or array grouping, where g=gcd(d_i); an ordered map adds a log tau(g) factor to the triple loop. Memory is O(M+tau(g)).
tricks: Model the allowed operations as the finite translation group Z_a x Z_b x Z_c and use Burnside rather than dividing the total number of colorings by abc.
For one translation, distinguish its orbit length L from its number N/L of cell cycles.
The order of a three-coordinate translation is the lcm of its three coordinate orders.
Replace the condition L divides every d_i by the single check L divides gcd(d_1,...,d_k).
There are phi(p) shifts of order p in a cyclic coordinate of length a for every p dividing a.
Group divisor triples by their common L before spending O(k) time on the fixed-coloring multinomial.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains for the primary Burnside-totient variant after the independent proof, source cross-checks, all-sample verification, and exhaustive small-instance verification.

The Codeforces tutorial prose widget did not render in the fetched page; the official source did expose both complete accepted reference implementations, while the full explanatory derivation was read from Luogu and independently reconstructed.
The official divisor-lcm DP alternative was verified algebraically as another way to aggregate the same Burnside weights, but it was not separately reimplemented during this review.
math/combinatorics/burnside-lemma is absent from the current registry and is proposed as a reusable candidate; applying this payload later requires taxonomy approval and a WebUI translation.
proof_risks: L is the length of each cell orbit, not the number of orbits; the fixed multinomial uses N/L slots and d_i/L slots of color i.
Every orbit has the same length L because a translation acts regularly on the torus and the least positive power fixing one cell is exactly the group-element order.
The number of coordinate shifts with order p is phi(p), including phi(1)=1 for the zero shift; it is not the number of divisors with that order.
Colors remain labeled even when some d_i are equal, so the multinomial must not be divided by multiplicities of equal counts.
Burnside divides by the full group size abc, not by the number of distinct cycle lengths or divisor triples.

solution_variants.name: burnside-totient-divisor-enumeration
solution_variants.summary: Enumerate coordinate orders p|a, q|b, r|c, weight the resulting translation order L=lcm(p,q,r) by phi(p)phi(q)phi(r), attach the exact-multiplicity multinomial when L divides every color count, and average the fixed-point sum over all abc translations.
solution_variants.complexity: After O(M log M) preprocessing, O(sqrt(a)+sqrt(b)+sqrt(c)+tau(a)tau(b)tau(c)+k tau(g)) arithmetic per test with O(tau(g)) temporary memory, aside from the O(M) tables.

solution_variants.name: burnside-divisor-lcm-state-dp
solution_variants.summary: For each dimension, recover the number of shifts of every exact coordinate order by subtracting proper-multiple divisor counts. Then run three DP layers whose state is the current lcm and whose transition appends one coordinate order; use the resulting count of translations for each L in the same Burnside multinomial sum.
solution_variants.complexity: After factorial preprocessing, O(sqrt(a)+sqrt(b)+sqrt(c)+tau(a)^2+tau(b)^2+tau(c)^2+tau(g)^2+k tau(g)) time and O(tau(a)+tau(b)+tau(c)+tau(g)) temporary memory.

tags.evidence: The desired equivalence classes are exactly orbits under the abc-element translation group, and the answer is obtained by averaging the exact number of colorings fixed by every translation; unequal stabilizer sizes make this orbit-counting step indispensable.
tags.description: Count orbits of configurations under a finite group action by averaging the number of configurations fixed by each group element. Use when symmetry-equivalent objects are the target and fixed-point counts drive the solution.
tags.created_reason: CF2040F is a direct finite-group orbit-counting problem under Z_a x Z_b x Z_c. Existing generic combinatorics and multinomial tags do not identify symmetry counting by fixed-point averaging.

tags.evidence: For each divisor p of a, exactly phi(p) coordinate shifts x have order a/gcd(a,x)=p; the product phi(p)phi(q)phi(r) is the exact multiplicity of each divisor-order triple in Burnside's sum.
A translation of order L leaves N/L distinguishable cell cycles; assigning exactly d_i/L cycles to each labeled color gives the fixed-point count (N/L)!/product_i((d_i/L)!).
The orbit length of translation (x,y,z) is its group order L=lcm(a/gcd(a,x), b/gcd(b,y), c/gcd(c,z)); this L determines both feasibility and the multinomial fixed-point count.
Coordinate orders must divide a, b, and c, so enumerating those three divisor sets replaces an infeasible scan over all abc translations and supplies all Burnside groups.
A translation order contributes iff it divides every color multiplicity, compressed to the single condition L|g for g=gcd(d_1,...,d_k); coordinate orders are also derived as n/gcd(n,shift).

The official alternative uses three counting-DP layers over divisor-valued lcm states, where each transition appends one coordinate order with its exact number of shifts and accumulates the number of translation triples producing every final L.
